title: print() and Comments
description: Using print() to output text, writing comments, and identifying syntax and logic errors.

steps.title: Do Now Activity
steps.content:
Do Now Activity

5 minutes — from last lesson

Identify the error type in each line of code. Choose from Syntax Error, Logic Error, or No Error.

steps.title: The print() Function
steps.content:
The print() function

print() outputs text to the screen. It is one of the most used functions in Python.

print("Hello, World!")

Click run to see what happens

When this line runs, the screen shows:

Hello, World!

print() has three rules:

1. Lowercase

Python is case-sensitive. print must be lowercase. Print or PRINT will not work.

2. Parentheses

The text goes inside brackets: print("text"). Both the opening ( and closing ) are required.

3. Quotation marks

Text must be wrapped in quote marks: "like this". Both the opening and closing quote are required.

You can print words, numbers, or sentences — anything inside the quotes appears on screen exactly as written.

steps.title: Comments
steps.content:
Comments

A comment is a note in your code that Python ignores when the program runs. Comments start with a # symbol.

# Output the player's name
print("Alice")

Click run to see what happens

The comment on line 1 describes what the code does. Python skips it. Only the print() line actually runs.

Comments are used to:

  • Explain what a line of code does
  • Help another person reading your code understand it
  • Help you remember what you wrote weeks later
Valid comment
# Display the score
Not a comment (JavaScript style)
// Display the score
Not a comment (HTML style)
<!-- Display the score -->

Python only uses # for comments.

steps.title: Syntax Errors and Logic Errors
steps.content:
Syntax Errors and Logic Errors

When code has a problem, it contains an error. There are two types you need to know:

Syntax Error

A mistake in the structure of the code. Python cannot run the line at all.

Print("Hello")
print("Hello"
print "Hello")

Capital P, missing closing bracket, missing opening bracket

Click run to see what happens

Logic Error

The code runs without crashing, but the output is wrong. Usually a spelling mistake inside the quotes.

print("Helo")
print("Wrold")

Python runs these fine, but the output is wrong because the strings are misspelled

Click run to see what happens

Key difference: A syntax error stops the program from running. A logic error lets the program run but produces the wrong result.
